en.course.section.1_0.title: General Security Concepts
en.course.section.1_0.subsection.1_1.title: Security Controls
Security control categories define how controls are implemented and managed within an organization. They help classify security measures based on their nature and responsibility.
Controls implemented using hardware or software to protect systems, networks, and data from unauthorized access or attacks.
Controls focused on governance, policies, procedures, and risk management decisions. These controls define how security is managed at an organizational level.
Controls carried out by people through processes and procedures. These ensure day-to-day security operations are effective.
Controls designed to protect physical assets, facilities, and infrastructure from unauthorized access or damage.
Which category includes firewalls and encryption?
Technical controls rely on hardware and software mechanisms.
Security policies and governance belong to which category?
Managerial controls define rules and risk management strategies.
An incident response procedure is an example of:
Operational controls are performed by people.
Security cameras and guards are part of:
They protect physical assets and locations.
Which category focuses on risk assessment and compliance?
Risk management is a managerial responsibility.
Control types define the purpose of a control in preventing, detecting, or responding to security threats. They describe how a control behaves in the security lifecycle.
Controls that stop security incidents before they occur.
Controls that discourage attackers from attempting an attack.
Controls that identify and alert on security incidents after they occur.
Controls that fix or restore systems after an incident.
Alternative controls used when a primary control is not feasible.
Controls that provide guidance and enforce policies or procedures.
A firewall is an example of which control type?
It blocks unauthorized access before it happens.
A visible security camera that discourages theft is:
An IDS that alerts on suspicious activity is:
It identifies attacks after they occur.
It restores systems after an incident.
Using MFA because passwords alone are weak is:
It compensates for a weak primary control.
Which control type is BEST for reducing the likelihood of an attack?
Preventive controls stop attacks before they occur.
Which control type is used to identify a breach in progress?
en.course.section.1_0.subsection.1_2.title: Fundamental Security Concepts
The CIA triad represents the three core principles of information security: confidentiality, integrity, and availability.
Ensures that data is only accessible to authorized users.
Ensures data is accurate, consistent, and not modified improperly.
Ensures systems and data are accessible when needed.
It verifies data has not been altered.
Non-repudiation ensures that a user cannot deny performing an action.
Provides proof of origin and integrity so actions cannot be denied.
Digital signatures prove identity and origin.
AAA stands for Authentication, Authorization, and Accounting, which together control access and track activity.
Verifies the identity of users or systems.
Methods used to verify a human user's identity (something you know/are/have/do/where you are).
Methods used to verify devices/services (machine identity) before allowing access.
Determines what actions a user is allowed to perform.
Ways to define and enforce access decisions based on roles, attributes, or rules.
Gap analysis identifies the difference between current security posture and desired state.
Compares current controls with required controls to identify missing protections.
What is the purpose of gap analysis?
Zero Trust is a security model that assumes no implicit trust and requires continuous verification.
Adjusts authentication requirements based on risk.
Reduces the blast radius of an attacker by limiting access paths and exposure.
Access decisions are made dynamically based on context and policies instead of static network location.
Enforces policies from the policy engine.
Traditional 'trusted' network areas assumed safe; Zero Trust aims to eliminate these assumptions.
Physical security protects facilities and infrastructure.
Mantrap-style entry area to prevent tailgating and enforce access control.
Detect physical intrusion attempts and trigger alerts.
Uses microwave signals to detect motion.
Uses ultrasonic waves to detect movement.
Deception technologies are used to mislead attackers and detect malicious activity.
Fake file used to detect access.
Fake data used to detect misuse.
en.course.section.2.title: reats, Vulnerabilities, and Mitigations
en.course.section.2.subsection.2_1.title: Threat Actors and Motivations
en.course.section.2.subsection.2_1.topic.ac: Threat actors are individuals or groups that carry out malicious activities against systems or organizations.
Government-sponsored actors conducting cyber operations for political, military, or economic objectives.
Individuals with little technical knowledge using pre-built tools.
Actors motivated by political or ideological beliefs.
Trusted individuals who misuse access intentionally or unintentionally.
Highly structured groups focused on financial gain.
Unauthorized systems or applications used within an organization.
Which threat actor is most likely to have high funding and resources?
Nation-states have significant resources and funding.
They use pre-made tools without deep understanding.
Which actor is most likely to launch ransomware for profit?
en.course.section.2.subsection.2_1.topic.at: Threat actors can be classified based on their origin, resources, and level of sophistication.
Internal actors operate within the organization, while external actors attack from outside.
The amount of financial and technological resources available.
The skill level and technical capability of the attacker.
Which actor type has the highest level of sophistication?
An employee accessing sensitive data is:
More skilled attackers perform complex attacks.
en.course.section.2.subsection.2_1.topic.mo: Threat actors are driven by different motivations that influence their targets and attack methods.
Threatening to release data unless paid.
A hacktivist attack is driven by:
An employee leaking data due to anger is motivated by:
en.course.section.2.subsection.2_2.title: Threat Vectors and Attack Surfaces
en.course.section.2.subsection.2_2.topic.me: Attackers use messaging platforms to deliver malicious content or deceive users into performing unsafe actions.
Common vector used to deliver phishing attacks or malicious attachments.
Used for smishing attacks with malicious links.
Messaging apps used to spread malicious links or impersonate users.
Which vector is most commonly used for phishing?
Email is the most common phishing vector.
en.course.section.2.subsection.2_2.topic.fi: Attackers embed malicious code inside files or images to exploit vulnerabilities.
Malicious files such as PDFs or executables used to infect systems.
Malware hidden in image files or exploiting image parsing vulnerabilities.
Malicious PDF is an example of:
It is delivered as a file.
en.course.section.2.subsection.2_2.topic.ot: Various attack vectors that rely on physical devices or communication methods.
Attack via phone calls to manipulate users.
Malicious USB devices used to infect systems.
en.course.section.2.subsection.2_2.topic.so: Software vulnerabilities can be exploited by attackers.
en.course.section.2.subsection.2_2.topic.ne: Insecure networks can expose systems to attacks.
Physical networks can be tapped or misconfigured.
Which network is most vulnerable to eavesdropping?
en.course.section.2.subsection.2_2.topic.mi: Default usernames and passwords left unchanged.
en.course.section.2.subsection.2_2.topic.su: Third-party providers can introduce risks into systems.
en.course.section.2.subsection.2_2.topic.so: Social engineering exploits human behavior to gain access or information.
Creating a fake scenario to obtain information.
Fake domains similar to real ones.
en.course.section.2.subsection.2_3.title: Vulnerabilities
en.course.section.2.subsection.2_3.topic.ap: Application vulnerabilities originate from insecure coding practices or logic flaws.
Injecting malicious code into process memory.
Writing data beyond buffer limits causing memory corruption.
Validation occurs before use, allowing manipulation.
System state changes between check and execution.
en.course.section.2.subsection.2_3.topic.os: Vulnerabilities related to flaws or misconfigurations in operating systems.
Weaknesses in OS design or unpatched systems.
en.course.section.2.subsection.2_3.topic.we: Web application vulnerabilities targeting browsers or web servers.
Injecting malicious SQL queries into input fields.
en.course.section.2.subsection.2_3.topic.ha: Physical device vulnerabilities that can be exploited.
Low-level software that may contain vulnerabilities.
Devices no longer supported with updates.
en.course.section.2.subsection.2_3.topic.vi: Breaking out of a VM to access host system.
Improper cleanup of resources shared between VMs.
en.course.section.2.subsection.2_3.topic.ze: Unknown vulnerabilities with no available patch.
New vulnerability not yet known or patched.
en.course.section.2.subsection.2_4.title: Indicators of Malicious Activity
en.course.section.2.subsection.2_4.topic.ma: Malware refers to malicious software designed to harm systems or steal data.
Attaches to files and spreads when executed.
Hides malicious activity and maintains persistence.
Encrypted files and ransom note indicate:
Which malware hides itself deeply in system?
en.course.section.2.subsection.2_4.topic.ph: Attacks involving physical access or environmental manipulation.
Trying many combinations to gain access.
en.course.section.2.subsection.2_4.topic.ne: Attacks targeting network infrastructure or communication.
Using third-party servers to increase attack traffic.
Spoofing victim IP to redirect traffic.
Injecting harmful code in network traffic.
en.course.section.2.subsection.2_4.topic.ap: Attacks targeting application logic or input handling.
en.course.section.2.subsection.2_4.topic.cr: Finding two inputs with same hash.
en.course.section.2.subsection.2_4.topic.pa: Trying common passwords across many accounts.
Trying one password on many users is:
en.course.section.2.subsection.2_4.topic.in: Signs that indicate potential malicious activity.
Logins from distant locations in short time.
Login from two countries within minutes indicates:
High CPU usage unexpectedly may indicate:
en.course.section.2.subsection.2_5.title: Mitigation Techniques
en.course.section.2.subsection.2_5.topic.se: Segmentation divides networks into smaller zones to limit the spread of attacks.
en.course.section.2.subsection.2_5.topic.ac: Mechanisms used to restrict access to systems and data.
en.course.section.2.subsection.2_5.topic.al: Only approved applications are allowed to run.
en.course.section.2.subsection.2_5.topic.mo: Tracking system activity to detect threats.
en.course.section.3.title: 3.0 Security Architecture
en.course.section.3.subsection.3_1.title: Architecture Models and Security Implications
en.course.section.3.subsection.3_1.topic.ar: Different architecture models impact security, scalability, and operational risk.
Shared responsibility model between provider and customer.
Defines who is responsible for security controls.
Combination of cloud and on-prem systems.
No server management, code runs on demand.
Running multiple OS on one system.
Who is responsible for security in cloud?
Both provider and customer share responsibilities.
en.course.section.3.subsection.3_1.topic.co: Key factors when choosing architecture models.
en.course.section.3.subsection.3_2.title: Secure Enterprise Infrastructure
en.course.section.3.subsection.3_2.topic.in: Security depends on proper infrastructure design, placement, and control selection.
Strategic positioning of devices to control traffic.
Total exposure points available to attackers.
How systems communicate with each other.
Device placed directly in traffic path.
Which mode blocks traffic if system fails?
en.course.section.3.subsection.3_2.topic.ap: Security devices used to protect infrastructure.
Secure entry point for administrative access.
Detects attacks but does not block.
en.course.section.3.subsection.3_2.topic.po: Mechanisms to secure network access points.
en.course.section.3.subsection.3_2.topic.fi: Different firewalls provide varying levels of protection.
Filters based on IP and port.
en.course.section.3.subsection.3_2.topic.co: Techniques to secure remote access and communication.
Choosing the right controls based on risk and environment.
en.course.section.3.subsection.3_3.title: Data Protection Concepts and Strategies
en.course.section.3.subsection.3_3.topic.da: Different types of data require different protection levels based on sensitivity and legal requirements.
Data protected by laws and regulations.
Which data is protected by law?
en.course.section.3.subsection.3_3.topic.cl: Classification defines the sensitivity level of data.
Requires protection but not highly restricted.
en.course.section.3.subsection.3_3.topic.da: Data security depends on its state and location.
en.course.section.3.subsection.3_3.topic.me: Techniques used to protect data from unauthorized access.
en.course.section.3.subsection.3_4.title: Resilience and Recovery
en.course.section.3.subsection.3_4.topic.av: Ensuring systems remain operational with minimal downtime.
Group of systems working together for redundancy.
Ensures availability if one node fails.
en.course.section.3.subsection.3_4.topic.si: Different recovery site types based on readiness and cost.
Partially ready with some setup needed.
Which site is fastest to recover?
en.course.section.3.subsection.3_4.topic.pl: Using different systems to reduce common failure risk.
en.course.section.3.subsection.3_4.topic.mu: Using multiple cloud providers for resilience.
en.course.section.3.subsection.3_4.topic.po: Ensuring systems remain powered during outages.
en.course.section.4_0.title: 4.0 Security Operations
en.course.section.4_0.subsection.4_1.title: Secure Computing Resources
Baseline configurations ensure systems are deployed securely and consistently.
Different systems require specific hardening techniques.
Which system often cannot be patched easily?
Proper planning is required for secure wireless deployment.
en.course.section.4_0.subsection.4_2.title: Asset Management
The process of acquiring hardware, software, and data assets securely.
Ensuring assets are purchased securely and from trusted sources.
Assets must come from secure sources.
Tracking ownership and classification of assets.
Someone is responsible for the asset.
Tracking and monitoring assets throughout their lifecycle.
en.course.section.4_0.subsection.4_3.title: Vulnerability Management
Methods used to discover vulnerabilities in systems.
Automated tool to detect known vulnerabilities.
Reported issue that is not real.
Actions taken to fix or mitigate vulnerabilities.
Using firewall instead of patch is:
en.course.section.4_0.subsection.4_4.title: Security Alerting and Monitoring
Continuous observation of systems, applications, and infrastructure to detect abnormal behavior.
Core activities involved in security monitoring operations.
Generating alerts when suspicious events occur.
All logs are collected in one place.
Security tools used to detect, analyze, and respond to threats.
Monitoring with or without installed software.
Central platform for log analysis and correlation.
en.course.section.4_0.subsection.4_5.title: Security Enhancement
Firewalls control traffic based on rules to protect network boundaries.
List of permissions for traffic filtering.
IPS differs from IDS because it:
en.course.section.4_0.subsection.4_6.title: Identity and Access Management (IAM)
Managing the lifecycle of user accounts.
Creating and assigning access to users.
Removing access when no longer needed.
en.course.section.4_0.subsection.4_7.title: Automation and Orchestration
Use Cases of Automation and Scripting
Automation reduces manual work and enforces security processes consistently.
Automatically creating and managing user accounts.
Advantages of using automation in security operations.
Automation can be difficult to design.
en.course.section.4_0.subsection.4_8.title: Incident Response
Structured approach to handle security incidents.
Identifying the origin of the incident.
en.course.section.4_0.subsection.4_9.title: Given a scenario, use data sources to support an investigation
Logs provide detailed records of system and network activities used for investigations.
Records of allowed and blocked traffic.
Which log helps detect blocked attacks?
Additional sources used to support investigation and analysis.
Combining multiple data sources to understand incidents.
en.course.section.5_0.title: 5.0 Security Program Management and Oversight
en.course.section.5_0.subsection.5_1.title: Security Governance
Recommendations that guide behavior but are not mandatory.
High-level mandatory rules that define security expectations.
No personal use of company systems
Defines who manages and protects data.
Who is responsible for data classification?
en.course.section.5_0.subsection.5_2.title: Risk Management
Identifying potential risks that could impact the organization.
Analyzing the likelihood and impact of risks.
Defines how much risk an organization is willing to accept.
en.course.section.5_0.subsection.5_3.title: Third-Party Risk Management
Evaluating security posture of third parties before engagement.
SOC 2 report is an example of:
Choosing vendors based on risk and compliance.
Legal agreements defining responsibilities and expectations.
Ongoing evaluation of vendor performance and risk.
en.course.section.5_0.subsection.5_4.title: Security Compliance
Risks of failing to meet compliance requirements.
Ensuring ongoing adherence to compliance requirements.
Protecting personal data and complying with privacy laws.
en.course.section.5_0.subsection.5_5.title: Audits and Assessments
Formal confirmation that controls or processes meet requirements.
en.course.section.5_0.subsection.5_6.title: Security Awareness
Training users to identify and respond to phishing attempts.
What is the first action when receiving a suspicious email?
